title: Foundation Species
text:
Foundation species — such as mussels and seagrasses — are ecosystem engineers that create and modify habitats, driving both community assembly and large-scale ecosystem functions. We study how the geometry, connectivity, and fragmentation of these habitats shape ecological and biogeochemical processes.

Our work on mussel beds focuses on how habitat area, perimeter, and fragmentation affect nutrient fluxes (ammonium, oxygen) and community structure. We use experimental mussel transplants and metabolic chambers to quantify fluxes at the whole-ecosystem level.
Our work on seagrass meadows and detritus develops theory for the dual role of detritus as both a resource and a habitat, integrating non-trophic processes into meta-ecosystem dynamics.
We develop and test theories of meta-ecosystem engineers involving both trophic and non-trophic interactions, asking how the spatial flows and cycling of matter mediated by foundation species scale from local beds to seascape-level functions.



Guichard, F., Pichon, B., Gounand, I. (2026) The dual role of detritus as resource and habitat. Oikos, in press. Preprint
Lauzon, F., et al. (2026) Dynamic response of communities and ecosystem functions to ecosystem engineering and multiple stressors in a marine mussel bed. Oikos, in press.
Carrier-Belleau, C., et al. (2024) Interacting effects of local and global stressors on mussel beds and ecosystem functioning. Journal of Experimental Marine Biology and Ecology. DOI
Paquette, L., Archambault, P., Guichard, F. (2019) From habitat geometry to ecosystem functions in marine mussel beds. Marine Ecology Progress Series 608: 149–163.
Largaespada, C., Guichard, F., Archambault, P. (2012) Meta-ecosystem engineering: Nutrient fluxes reveal intra-specific and inter-specific feedbacks in fragmented mussel beds. Ecology 93: 324–333.

title: Meta-ecosystems
text:
Meta-ecosystems extend metacommunity theory by explicitly incorporating the spatial flows of matter and energy — not just organisms — across habitat patches. We develop and test meta-ecosystem theory to understand how nutrient flows, detritus, and consumer movement shape ecosystem functions across landscapes.
Our theoretical work addresses:

How resource flows between ecosystems can destabilize food chains or generate emergent nutrient co-limitation
The role of mobile consumers in recycling nutrients and mediating trophic regulation across space
Partial migration and its significance for food-web and ecosystem dynamics
The emergence of source-sink dynamics at the meta-ecosystem level

We apply these frameworks to both marine (mussel beds, seagrass, coral reefs) and freshwater systems, and connect theory to empirical data through collaborations across Canada and Europe.



Peller, T., Gounand, I., Fortin, M-J., Guichard, F. (2026) Spatially-nested topologies stabilize meta-ecosystems via cross-scale source-sink dynamics. Ecology, in press.
Peller, T., Guichard, F., Altermatt, F. (2023) The significance of partial migration for food-web and ecosystem dynamics. Ecology Letters 26(1): 3–22. DOI
Peller, T., Marleau, J., Guichard, F. (2022) Traits affecting nutrient recycling by mobile consumers can explain coexistence and spatially-heterogeneous trophic regulation across a meta-ecosystem. Ecology Letters 25: 440–452. DOI
Harvey, E., et al. (2023) A general meta-ecosystem model to predict ecosystem functions at landscape extents. Ecography. DOI
Marleau, J., Guichard, F., Loreau, M. (2015) Emergence of nutrient colimitation through movement in stoichiometric metaecosystems. Ecology Letters 18: 1163–1173.
Guichard, F. and Marleau, J. (2021) Meta-ecosystem Dynamics. Springer.

title: News
text:
Updates from the lab will appear here. Check back for new publications, lab member news, and upcoming events.

2025

Brendon McGuinness completed his PhD — congratulations! His paper on resource-use plasticity and microbial community structure was published in PNAS.
New paper: Townsend, Gouhier & Guichard on aggregated dispersal and spatial synchrony, published in Oikos.

2024

New paper: Arumugam, Guichard & Lutscher on early warning indicators of catastrophic transitions, published in Ecology.
New paper: Hutchison, Gravel & Guichard on resilience of community dynamics with seasonal migrations, published in Theoretical Ecology.
New paper: Carrier-Belleau et al. on interacting stressors and mussel bed ecosystem functioning, published in Journal of Experimental Marine Biology and Ecology.

2023

New paper: Harvey et al. — a general meta-ecosystem model for predicting ecosystem functions at landscape extents, published in Ecography.
New paper: Peller, Guichard & Altermatt on partial migration and food-web dynamics, published in Ecology Letters.

Prospective graduate students and postdocs are welcome to get in touch by email.

title: Marine Connectivity
text:
Larval dispersal connects marine populations across space, determining the persistence, resilience, and recovery potential of marine metapopulations. We study how dispersal traits, oceanographic forcing, and the design of marine protected area (MPA) networks interact to shape metapopulation dynamics under current and future climate conditions.
Key questions include:

How do dispersal traits interact with dynamic connectivity to affect metapopulation growth and stability?
How should MPA networks be designed to account for non-equilibrium metacommunity dynamics and climate change?
How does aggregated dispersal affect spatial synchrony, stability, and extinction risk?

We develop spatial models linking larval dispersal behaviour, oceanographic variability, and population dynamics, and apply these to practical MPA design problems.

title: Marine Connectivity
text:
Larval dispersal connects marine populations across space, determining the persistence, resilience, and recovery potential of marine metapopulations. We study how dispersal traits, oceanographic forcing, and the design of marine protected area (MPA) networks interact to shape metapopulation dynamics under current and future climate conditions.
Key questions include:

How do dispersal traits interact with dynamic connectivity to affect metapopulation growth and stability?
How should MPA networks be designed to account for non-equilibrium metacommunity dynamics and climate change?
How does aggregated dispersal affect spatial synchrony, stability, and extinction risk?

We develop spatial models linking larval dispersal behaviour, oceanographic variability, and population dynamics, and apply these to practical MPA design problems.

title: Guichard Lab
text:
We are a theoretical and marine ecology group in the Department of Biology at McGill University. Our research focuses on the study of ecosystems as complex systems and on the problem of scale. We are interested in understanding:

How large-scale patterns of biological diversity develop and are maintained from local interactions among individuals
The maintenance of ecosystem functions by the limited movement of individuals and (in)organic matter
The role of non-equilibrium dynamics and strong variability for population persistence, community structure, and ecosystem functions
The maintenance of asynchrony across coupled ecological networks

We address these questions by combining field experiments in marine systems with theoretical approaches in order to link patterns and processes across scales. We develop and test theories of metacommunities and meta-ecosystems, and apply their predictions to the design of marine reserve networks.

Affiliated with:

title: About
text: About this site

title: Climate Change
text:
Our research investigates how ecosystems respond to continuously changing environmental conditions — not just to static stressors. A key focus is the concept of rate-dependent dynamics: how the speed of environmental change determines whether ecosystems can track shifting equilibria or instead cross tipping points into alternative states.
We combine theory (dynamical systems, bifurcation analysis) with metacommunity and meta-ecosystem models to study:

Early warning indicators of catastrophic transitions driven by explicit rates of environmental change
The persistence and extinction dynamics of predator-prey metacommunities under changing environments
Seasonal food webs and migrations under climate change, including indirect species interactions in Arctic tundra systems
The resilience of community dynamics with seasonal migrations




Arumugam, R., Guichard, F., Lutscher, F. (2024) Early warning indicators capture catastrophic transitions driven by explicit rates of environmental change. Ecology. DOI
Arumugam, R., Lutscher, F., Guichard, F. (2021) Tracking unstable states: Ecosystem dynamics in a changing world. Oikos 130: 525–540. DOI
Hutchison, C., Gravel, D., Guichard, F. (2024) A hybrid dynamical system approach to predicting the resilience of community dynamics with seasonal migrations under climate change. Theoretical Ecology. DOI
Hutchison, C., et al. (2020) Seasonal food webs with migrations. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A. DOI

title: Synchrony
text:
Spatial synchrony — correlated fluctuations in population dynamics across distant locations — has profound consequences for the persistence and stability of ecological systems. We study the mechanisms generating and maintaining synchrony and asynchrony across ecological networks, from marine shores to meta-ecosystems.
Our work spans:

The role of dispersal, environmental forcing, and trophic interactions in generating synchrony across metacommunities
How asynchrony among populations and species buffers communities against extinction (the portfolio effect)
Phase dynamics in heterogeneous metacommunities, including amplitude-frequency modulation
The maintenance of asynchrony across coupled ecological networks as a stabilizing mechanism

We develop both theoretical models and quantitative methods (see the synchrony R package) to characterize spatial variability across scales.



Milne, R., Guichard, F. (2021) Coupled phase-amplitude dynamics in heterogeneous metacommunities. Journal of Theoretical Biology 523: 110676. DOI
Guichard, F., Zhang, Y., Lutscher, F. (2019) The emergence of phase asynchrony and frequency modulation in metacommunities. Theoretical Ecology 12: 329. DOI
Gouhier, T.C. and Guichard, F. (2014) Synchrony: quantifying variability in space and time. Methods in Ecology and Evolution 5: 524–533.
Gouhier, T.C., Guichard, F., Gonzalez, A. (2010) Synchrony and stability of food webs in metacommunities. The American Naturalist 175: E16–E34.
Gouhier, T.C., Guichard, F., Menge, B.A. (2010) Ecological processes can synchronize marine population dynamics over continental scales. PNAS 107: 8281–8286.
Wall, E., Guichard, F., Humphries, A.R. (2013) Synchronization in ecological systems by weak dispersal coupling with time delay. Theoretical Ecology 6: 405–418.
